moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the Zukertort Opening, a flexible first move that develops our knight to its ideal square while keeping our options open—we haven't committed to a central pawn structure yet. Nf3 controls the important central squares e5 and d4, supports a future e4 advance, and allows us to transpose into many different openings depending on Black's response. This move embodies the principles of quiet, flexible play that characterized Zukertort's style: we develop purposefully without revealing our full plan.

Black plays the Polish Defense, an unusual and somewhat provocative response that immediately stakes a claim on the queenside rather than challenging the center. This move prepares ...b4 to attack our knight and gains space on the flank, but it weakens Black's kingside slightly and commits to a plan before we've even stated our intentions. While not objectively bad, b5 is more of a chess puzzle than a mainstream opening—Black is betting on unconventional ideas, so we should respond with solid, central development rather than getting drawn into queenside complications.
